data.title: Why did the weaponization of the US dollar fail in the Iran war?
data.description: The Strait of Hormuz crisis is revealing inherent cracks in America's most powerful geopolitical tool: the weaponization of the dollar is least effective against adversaries in need of pressure, while the overused sanctions system has now become a force multiplier for America's adversaries. Recent developments show that after the announcement of the two-week ceasefire between the US and Iran, risk aversion subsided, putting pressure on the dollar index. The ICE dollar index once plunged 1.2%, erasing all gains for the year, while the Bloomberg dollar spot index fell 0.8%, its worst single-day performance since January. Media reports on the 8th stated that Iranian officials indicated they would require shipping companies to pay transit fees for oil tankers in cryptocurrency, at a rate of $1 per barrel of crude oil transported. Prior to this, according to Lloyd’s List Intelligence, merchant ships had already paid up to $2 million to Iran to ensure safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz. According to Daniel Davies, Managing Director of Frontline Analysts and author of *The Unaccountability Machine*, Iran's move to cryptocurrency pricing signifies a structural challenge to the dollar-based sanctions system. When sanctioned countries can bypass dollar clearing channels and exercise de facto pricing power over international shipping, the geopolitical deterrent power supported by dollar hegemony will be substantially weakened, and its long-term impact on the global financial order cannot be ignored. He cites a central banker's maxim from David Kynaston's *History of the Bank of England* as a warning: "You can wield the stick if you want, but never actually use it; it may break in your hand. It's better to raise a finger as a warning." In Daniel Davies' view, the Hormuz crisis may become a watershed moment in history—the dollar's big stick is already in grave danger. Two Historical Clues to the Ineffectiveness of Sanctions Daniel Davies traces the loosening of this logic back to 2022. At that time, Russian banks were sanctioned and cut off from the SWIFT international payment system. Even then, it was widely predicted that this would be more of an "inconvenience" than an "economic death sentence" for Russia. However, Russia's continued war-fighting capabilities and oil export revenues afterward greatly disappointed the sanctions supporters. The case of Iran is even more convincing. Daniel Davies states that Iran is one of the few countries subject to nationwide sanctions by the US Treasury Department, but this neither prevented it from continuing to sell oil during its war with the US, nor prevented it from charging "transit fees" to international merchant ships intending to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The existence of sanctions did not change Iran's actual capacity for action. The Inherent Paradox of the Dollar Weapon: According to Daniel Davies, the root of the problem lies in a structural paradox: the deterrent power of dollar sanctions stems precisely from the convenience and profitability of the dollar economic system itself. This means that this weapon is most effective against open economies deeply integrated into the global supply chain—and these countries are rarely the ones truly in need of pressure. Countries subject to long-term sanctions are quite different; they are often accustomed to "finding what they have locally" and have built networks of alternative routes. According to research by the Atlantic Council, there are also a number of banks and shadow financial institutions willing to bear the risks of US extraterritorial law enforcement to provide laundering channels for dollar payments—these counterparties are not so dependent on access to the New York dollar clearing system. In today's increasingly diverse landscape of anonymous internet payments, these alternative channels are gradually becoming the norm. The US's inability to completely block virtual currency or digital asset transactions linked to real-world assets through decentralized networks makes cross-border payment flows more covert. Ironically, the extremely cumbersome anti-money laundering compliance requirements imposed on its allies by the US have inadvertently driven sanctioned countries to accelerate their move into regulatory vacuums. He uses OPEC history as an analogy: since its inception, OPEC member countries have long understood that providing users of their products with the incentive to find alternatives is an extremely unwise strategy. The US's excessive weaponization of the dollar system is repeating this mistake. Daniel Davies points out that this trend was predicted academically. He and political scientist Henry Farrell—one of the co-proponents of the concept of "weaponized interdependence"—had already clearly articulated this in a paper published earlier this year. The two authors wrote, "As the US continues to escalate its pressure, other countries will seek to break free from the power of the dollar, which could very well prompt the US to further escalate its response." Their core judgment is that when the dollar is excessively weaponized, the global financial system has transformed from a geopolitical lever for the US into a force multiplier for its adversaries. The stick can be wielded, but the day it is used may also be the day it breaks.

data.title: Strait of Hormuz: Witnessing the Golden Age of Censorship Resistance for Crypto Assets
data.description: Yesterday I saw a news item on Sina Weibo (the gist of which was): Iran is demanding that oil tankers passing through the Strait of Hormuz pay their tolls in cryptocurrency. Upon seeing this news, my first reaction was that this cryptocurrency was most likely not a stablecoin like USDC or USDT. Then I wondered: why didn't the news mention a specific cryptocurrency? Then I looked at the news details again, which said no cryptocurrency was specified for payment. But then I checked international news websites, and they showed that Iran was demanding payment in Bitcoin. Why did my first thought be that it was unlikely to be one of those stablecoins? The reason is simple: because they are essentially centralized entities, and tightly controlled by the US government. For adversaries caught in intense political strife, using such assets could result in their stablecoin issuers freezing their accounts, leaving Iran with no recourse but to suffer in silence. The values ​​of freedom and censorship resistance are paramount and invaluable in this momentous historical conflict. They hold a supreme, intangible value not only for Iran but for all groups and individuals. For censorship-resistant assets like Bitcoin to build and demonstrate such value, and for the world to recognize it, requires repeated major historical events and dramatic conflicts that alter the course of history. The historical weight of gold was accumulated over time; crypto assets are clearly experiencing a similar historical process. I have always held this view: The most valuable things are those that both angels and devils can use and trust. This is like the air we depend on every moment. The Creator provides us with this substance without distinguishing who is qualified to breathe it, and does not deprive someone of their right to breathe air simply because they are a devil or have committed heinous crimes. As for whether there are other ways to prevent demons and heinous criminals from breathing the air, that's a human matter to be handled by humanity. The Creator should strive to remain neutral. Only by transcending such conflicts can the Creator's creations be recognized by all of humanity. The highest-level crypto assets should be viewed from the Creator's perspective, not presupposed by human values ​​and value orientations. I'm reminded of the Ethereum FOCIL mechanism I read about recently. In real-world scenarios, we inevitably encounter situations where some nodes, for whatever reason, in order to please regulators, will block certain accounts and block certain transactions at the regulators' request. This is what we often hear as the "blacklist" mechanism. To prevent this from happening, Ethereum's FOCIL mechanism establishes a "jury" of 17 nodes. These 17 nodes are randomly selected from the entire network. Each node contains a list of transactions that must be included in a block. If a block packager's block lacks any of the transactions listed by these 17 nodes, the resulting block will be deemed invalid and discarded by the entire network. With the FOCIL mechanism, unless a regulatory body can enforce oversight of all nodes on the network, it is difficult to interfere with Ethereum's neutrality through transaction blocking. Regarding the FOCIL mechanism, Vitalik also mentioned that this mechanism will be further strengthened in the future to solidify Ethereum's censorship resistance and make it as neutral as possible.

data.title: MEV Reshaping: Towards Transaction-Oriented Value
data.description: The term MEV no longer accurately describes the current situation, and the author proposes renaming it "Transaction Ordered Value (TOV)." Most ordering behavior is a normal market manifestation of block space pricing, and through technological innovations in chains like Solana, malicious MEVs have been significantly reduced. Current blockchain systems are comparable to traditional finance in terms of execution quality. We can examine the history of blockchain engineering from the perspective of antifragility or hormesis. Both terms describe a function where early constraints or setbacks actually improve the long-term performance of the system. This phenomenon succinctly summarizes the evolution of blockchain over 15 years: from a system designed by Satoshi Nakamoto that took 10 minutes to settle transactions, to fast, smart contract-enabled blockchains like Solana. Today, these systems can confidently claim to rival SWIFT, Nasdaq, ACH, and other systems that underpin modern financial life. As an engineer, my focus is on enabling systems like Solana to surpass traditional competitors while preserving the unique attributes of blockchain as a technology. The current excellence of blockchain networks is the result of years of effort to master its permissionless nature. This uniqueness brings concrete advantages such as deep liquidity, efficiency, and fast settlement. Despite its continued success, the evolution of blockchain is easily overlooked in some aspects. For example, at a recent digital asset summit in New York, some advocates of permissioned blockchains (consortium blockchains) made comments. In short, some speakers argued that decentralized blockchain networks like Ethereum and Solana are unsuitable for "traditional financial instruments" due to the widespread presence of malicious MEVs. From my perspective, which deals with these issues daily, this criticism seems outdated and out of touch—it's tantamount to arguing in 2026 that cryptocurrencies and blockchain technology will only be used by the black market and criminals. In fact, I believe it's time to raise awareness: "MEV" as a term is functionally ineffective. MEV is largely a solved problem, and engineers should focus on improving the utility of TOV (Transaction Ordering Value). The Evolution and Limitations of MEV To understand why the term "MEV" is outdated, it's worth revisiting its origins. MEV was initially defined in the Proof-of-Work (PoW) era as "Miner Extractable Value," referring to a miner's ability to reorder, include, or exclude transactions within a block for profit. As networks evolved to non-mining models, the abbreviation was redefined as "Maximal Extractable Value"—one of many examples of the term itself failing to keep pace with underlying technological advancements. Over time, "MEV" has become a broad term, often used imprecisely to describe everything from benign arbitrage to malicious attacks like sandwich attacks. This leads to a misclassification: not all transaction ordering is harmful; most is crucial for the operation of an efficient market. The value generated by transaction ordering should more accurately be understood as Transaction Order Value (TOV)—the economic outcome of allocating scarce block space in a competitive system. This redefinition is critical. Block space is a finite resource, and like any scarce commodity, it must be allocated through a pricing mechanism. Base fees, priority fees, and optional tips are not distortions, but rather the expected performance of the system, ensuring that time-sensitive transactions are processed quickly while preventing spam and compensating validators. From this perspective, many behaviors labeled "MEV" are simply the market's own clearing process. The Transparency and Marketization of Transaction Ordering The key point is that this dynamic is not unique to cryptocurrencies. Traditional financial markets are rife with similar practices: companies pay for faster data feeds, custodial services, and lower-latency execution to gain an advantage. The difference is that in permissionless systems, these dynamics are transparent and observable in real time, rather than hidden behind private infrastructure. This is not to say that unfavorable transaction ordering doesn't exist. Strategies like front-running, back-running, or sandwich attacks do incur costs for users. However, with the rapid maturation of mitigation technologies, their prevalence and impact are often exaggerated. Today, users can set slippage tolerances, route transactions through private paths, or benefit from bulk auctions and cryptographic ordering schemes, all of which significantly reduce the space for exploitation. Defense Mechanisms and Innovations on Solana Especially on Solana, an additional layer of defense has emerged at the market layer. The absence of a public mempool, more efficient applications (such as proprietary AMMs), transparent dashboards from teams like Jito and Ghost Logs, coupled with proactive anti-spam measures and evolving validator specifications, have significantly reduced the behaviors that once dominated the MEV discussion. Meanwhile, innovations such as private transaction routing, trusted execution environments (such as Jito's block assembly market), and multiple concurrent proposers ensure that transactions remain confidential before execution, making predatory strategies more difficult to implement. As a result, the landscape is vastly different now than it was a few years ago. Malicious extractions now account for only a small fraction of block space activity, while the majority of transaction ranking value reflects legitimate competition for inclusion rights and speed. Equally important, user protections on leading networks are increasingly similar to those in traditional finance, offering substantial guarantees in terms of execution quality and settlement reliability, while still retaining the decentralized and permissionless characteristics unique to blockchains. In other words, problems once considered fatal flaws are now better understood as a controllable and, in many cases, beneficial characteristic of open financial systems. Looking to the future: From MEV to TOV Traditional MEV is no longer an effective perspective for evaluating blockchain systems. The underlying challenges haven't disappeared, but they have been broken down, measured, and significantly mitigated through a combination of market design and technological innovation. What remains is Transaction Order Value (TOV): a neutral, economically based concept that reflects how scarce block space is allocated in a competitive environment. Like many challenges in blockchain history, MEV initially seemed like an inevitable consequence of on-chain markets. However, thanks to the efforts of many excellent teams in the industry, malicious behavior is increasingly being mitigated and replaced by transparent, efficient market-layer processes. Therefore, to those of you working on permissioned blockchain systems: I believe you will find it a relief that you now have to find a new angle or limitation to point out to users. And then, we will solve that problem.

data.title: Binance's predicament
data.description:
Binance Life offers a rare insider perspective. From CZ's own writing, we can see the most authentic cracks in this world's largest cryptocurrency exchange. He packaged almost all the crises as "We survived." His tone was restrained, and his narration was light. But behind those understated paragraphs lie several wounds that have never truly healed. These predicaments are not unique to Binance; they are a bill that the entire crypto industry must inevitably pay as it transitions from a period of unregulated growth to one of stricter regulation. Seven Years of Exile: Shanghai, Tokyo, Malta, Singapore, Dubai. What appears to be a roadmap for global expansion is, in reality, a history of exile by regulatory bodies.

2018Year4Month,CZ made a special trip to Bermuda to meet with the Prime MinisterDavid Burt

Management based on trust

text="">8Point

In the Asia region, the regional headLeonWithout fully reporting, he unilaterally signed a contract to advance tens of millions of dollars in losses for users, with an additional clause stating that if the funds are recovered in the future, the proceeds will belong to the original management. Even more absurdly, the local board of directors has already signed off on it, including two members of Binance's management team. Someone within the company was simultaneously on both the buyer's and seller's sides in the same transaction. It was only later discovered that CZ concluded: "Binance expanded too quickly, and many people with impure motives infiltrated the team; management couldn't keep up." When a company expands to thousands of people in a very short period, management methods built on trust and value-based screening begin to fail.

2025At the end of the year, He Yi officially took over as UnitedCEO, tied withRichard Tengtied——

It's easier to ask for forgiveness than permission

“It's easier to ask for forgiveness than permission.”This quote comes from Richard·

2019Year text="">CZ5month, GenslerReturnedCZForwarded what students wroteBNBFeasibility analysis report,

CZ

2026In 2026, an Australian subsidiary was still fined AUD 10 million for misclassifying customersMiCAThe EU MiCA license application is still pending. It means that every subsequent license application and every bank negotiation must be accompanied by this record. A guilty plea is the end, but also a beginning in another sense. He later said that if he hadn't been greedy for the huge size of the US market in his early years, he might not have suffered such a costly reckoning. Highly Personalized Credit Endorsements In November 2023, before deciding to fly to the United States to plead guilty, CZ... The text="">held its last online meeting:“If I don't go, the Biden administration will kill Binance,BNB holderswill be severely damaged, and the industry may regress to10"

2023

YearSilvergate, Signature and other crypto-friendly banks have collapsed one after another, leavingCoinbase、

data.title: Delphi Digital: Encrypted privacy is taking shape
data.description:
Author: Delphi Digital Source: X, @Delphi_Digital Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

For years, on-chain privacy has been caught between two major challenges: cryptographic technology has not yet matured to the point of commercial application, while regulators regard all privacy tools as criminal infrastructure. In the past year, both of these aspects have changed, and a usable privacy technology stack has quietly taken shape in the cracks.

Privacy Pools and Compliance Issues

Currently, the most mature and deployed basic component is the privacy pool. Users deposit assets into a public fund pool, and then withdraw funds using zero-knowledge proofs, proving that they have legitimate deposits without needing to disclose which specific transaction. The verifier, besides knowing that the withdrawal is legitimate and valid, cannot obtain any other information, and the connection between the sender and receiver is also severed at the fund pool level.

The strength of privacy protection is directly linked to the number of participants. A privacy pool with only 100 deposits offers weak cover because the scope can be significantly narrowed down through time analysis; while a privacy pool with millions of deposits makes transaction correlation almost impossible to trace. ZEC has validated this model, with its privacy address supply surging from 11% to over 30% of the total supply within a year. On the EVM chain, Railgun has processed a cumulative transaction volume of $4.5 billion. The privacy pool solution addresses the regulatory concerns that have long plagued privacy protocols. A verifiable subset of clean deposits is maintained by an associated collection provider. Your withdrawal proofs will show that your funds belong to this compliant collection, allowing you to remain anonymous within the pool while the entire pool itself is auditable. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) allows smart contracts to compute on encrypted inputs and directly return encrypted outputs, without requiring decryption. Validators process transactions and update on-chain state, and no party in the system can see the underlying true values. FHE eliminates trust dependencies by keeping data encrypted throughout execution. Zama launched its first commercial-grade FHE deployment on Ethereum at the end of last year, enabling confidential stablecoin transfers based on cUSDT. A few weeks later, the protocol used FHE to encrypt bid amounts, completing a sealed-bid Dutch auction token sale, putting the technology stack under the pressure of real traffic from thousands of participants. FHE protects internal transaction data but cannot handle the transaction graph itself: even if the amounts are hidden, information such as who transacted with whom, when the transactions occurred, and the transaction frequency are still visible. To achieve complete privacy, FHE needs to be layered on top of a privacy pool. Only then will this technology stack truly become a system, rather than a collection of fragmented, isolated solutions. The Dilemma of Active Choice: All the aforementioned privacy technologies are available, but they all share a common characteristic: users must actively choose to enable them. Users must manually privatize their assets or deposit them into a privacy pool. However, the default state of all mainstream public blockchains is complete transparency, a default setting that results in a consistently small anonymity pool. Small pool → weak privacy → fewer users → further shrinking pool, creating a vicious cycle. Monero is the only public blockchain that makes privacy a mandatory default, and its experience explains why no other public blockchain has followed suit: it has been delisted from exchanges multiple times, and EU anti-money laundering regulations plan to ban privacy coin-related services on licensed exchanges. The price of mandatory privacy is the loss of institutional access, which may be why other mainstream public blockchains insist on optional privacy. The Ethereum Foundation announced Project Kohaku in October 2025 and demonstrated it at Devconnect the following month. This was the first serious attempt to push privacy capabilities down to the wallet layer. This toolkit provides wallet developers with a ready-to-use SDK that enables:

  • Privacy transfers based on Railgun

  • Isolated accounts by dApp to prevent cross-application tracking

  • Peer-to-peer transaction broadcasting to bypass centralized RPC service providers

Wallets built on Kohaku do not ask users if they need privacy; instead, they have built-in privacy features.

However, whether Kohaku can truly change the default behavior remains to be seen. A toolkit does not equate to widespread adoption. Wallet teams still need to integrate it, users still need to choose these types of wallets, and the underlying anonymous set must be large enough for privacy protection to be truly effective.

The technology to make privacy a default setting already exists: cryptography has been validated, implementations are online, the regulatory environment has shifted, and for the first time, a mainstream public blockchain foundation has proactively incorporated privacy into its underlying technology stack. Whether this is enough to break the cycle of "must be actively enabled," or whether on-chain privacy will remain a niche, proprietary tool, remains to be seen.

data.title: Cracking Bitcoin private keys in 9 minutes? A survival guide for blockchain under the quantum threat.
data.description:
On March 31, 2026, an ordinary Monday, the crypto world suddenly exploded. Google's quantum AI team published a paper stating that a quantum computer could crack a Bitcoin private key in just 9 minutes, while the average confirmation time for a Bitcoin block is 10 minutes. Some say this is alarmist, others say it's a long way from reality, but this time it's Google issuing the warning. Can quantum computers really crack Bitcoin? Is the threat real or exaggerated? What should ordinary people do? This article attempts to clarify this matter. Ordinary users do not need to panic at present, but should check the address format (avoid using Taproot addresses starting with bc1p for a long time), develop the habit of "one address, one transaction", and pay attention to the wallet vendor's subsequent updates. What exactly did Google's paper say? The general consensus in the industry was that a quantum computer would need millions of qubits to crack Bitcoin's encryption algorithm. This number was ridiculously large, so everyone thought it would take at least several decades. But Google's paper reduced that number to less than 500,000. That's a 20-fold reduction. The paper presents a specific attack scenario: when you send a Bitcoin transaction, your public key is briefly exposed to the network, waiting to be included in a block. This window averages 10 minutes. According to Google's estimates, a sufficiently powerful quantum computer can deduce your private key from your public key within approximately 9 minutes, then forge a transaction with a higher miner fee, intercepting the funds before your original transaction is recorded on the blockchain, with a success rate of about 41%. Of course, the paper describes a fault-tolerant quantum computer with full error correction capabilities. Google's own Willow processor has only 105 physical qubits, while the paper requires 500,000. This is a difference of 446 times, so a quantum computer capable of cracking Bitcoin does not yet exist. Google's own goal is to complete the migration to post-quantum cryptography by 2029, which to some extent indicates when they believe the threat will become a reality. However, if this machine is ever built, the cost of cracking Bitcoin will be much lower than you think.

Quantum computers and ordinary computers, what are the differences?

But before discussing what this means, we need to clarify one question: What exactly is a quantum computer?

Ordinary computers process information using bits, and each bit has only two states: 0 or 1.

Ordinary computers process information using bits, and each bit has only two states: 0 or 1.

Any computation involves operating on these 0s and 1s. A 256-bit private key means there are 2²⁵⁶ possible combinations: even with all the computing power in the world combined, it would take longer than the age of the universe to brute-force it using classical computers. This is why Bitcoin has been secure for the past 15 years. Quantum computers use qubits, and the magic of qubits lies in their superposition state: they can be both 0 and 1 simultaneously. Eight qubits don't just represent one state, but can simultaneously represent 256 states. The more qubits there are, the exponentially greater the parallel processing capability. However, parallelism alone is not enough to pose a threat to Bitcoin. What truly makes quantum computers a threat to cryptography is the "Shor's algorithm," invented in 1994 by MIT mathematician Peter Shor. This algorithm is specifically designed to factor large integers and solve the elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem, two challenging problems that form the foundation of Bitcoin and Ethereum's private key security. For example, a traditional computer is like trying to find the exit in a maze, only able to try one path at a time; a quantum computer with Shor's algorithm is like being given a top-down view of the maze, allowing you to instantly know where the exit is. Bitcoin uses a signature algorithm called ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm), which operates on the secp256k1 curve. This system is impenetrable to classical computers, but Shor's algorithm can specifically break through the mathematical structure of elliptic curves.

What is the Bitcoin community doing

What do ordinary users need to do now

After saying all that, what can ordinary users do?

The answer isn't as complicated as you might think. Quantum computers can't crack your Bitcoin today, but there are a few things you can start paying attention to now.

The answer isn't as complicated as you might think. Quantum computers can't crack your Bitcoin today, but there are a few things you can start paying attention to now.

Check your address format

Open your wallet and check what the receiving address starts with. Addresses starting with "bc1p" are Taproot addresses, where the public key is embedded in the address itself by default, representing a high-risk format that is exposed long-term. If your assets are stored in such addresses and have never been touched, the risk is currently theoretical, but it's worth paying attention to the progress of BIP-360.

Open your wallet and check what the receiving address starts with.

data.title: Doubao "became sentient" overnight: Natural dialogue experience fully launched.
data.description:
The "mechanical feel" of AI interaction has disappeared! Today, Doubao unveiled its native full-duplex voice model, Seeduplex, which can not only listen and speak simultaneously, but also understand your pauses or hesitations while thinking. It's also highly resistant to noise, boasting maximum anti-interference capabilities. Finally, this day has arrived! AI voice interaction is finally feeling human. Today, ByteDance's Seed team quietly released the native full-duplex voice model, Seeduplex, which is now officially available and fully rolled out on the Doubao App. Note that this is a **full rollout**. It's not a beta test, not a limited release, and not a "coming soon" announcement. Doubao's voice interaction experience has always been unparalleled, even rivaling Luo Yonghao's (founder of NetEase) best. But starting today, from the moment hundreds of millions of Doubao users open the app, they will experience something unprecedented—AI will no longer be a dutiful student waiting for you to finish a sentence, press a button, and then speak again, but a "conversation partner" that listens and speaks simultaneously, waits for you to think, can be interrupted, and can even understand what you're talking about in a coffee shop. This is a major event that has been underestimated by the industry. It means that full-duplex voice technology has, for the first time, truly stepped out of the laboratory and achieved large-scale deployment in the industry. We immediately rushed to conduct several rounds of real-world testing. To be honest, after experiencing it, I only had one feeling: the "mechanical" feeling that was so awkward when talking to AI has finally disappeared. That AI that always "interrupted" me—it finally feels like a real person! Generally speaking, human interaction involves a lot of information overlap, interruptions, hesitation, and environmental noise. Traditional "half-duplex" voice AI often struggles in these complex scenarios—either it interrupts you when you pause to think, or it rambles incoherently in noisy environments. This time, while maintaining extremely fast response, Seeduplex has tackled the two tough challenges of "precise anti-interference" and "dynamic stop judgment." Getting started is very simple: just upgrade the Doubao App to the latest version, enter the app, and click the call button in the upper right corner to start a smooth "voice call" experience.

Scenario 1: Discussing itineraries in a coffee shop, precisely resisting interference

Imagine entering a bustling coffee shop and discussing weekend plans with Doubao.

In the background, the uncle at the next table was talking loudly on the phone, the waiters' orders were being announced incessantly, and the coffee machine was humming... Halfway through the conversation, I casually turned to the counter: "Hello, I'd like a latte, no sugar." In such a noisy environment, any voice assistant in the past would almost certainly have been a disaster—either it would have treated what was said as a new command and executed it earnestly, or it would have simply stopped, requiring the wake word to be repeated to continue the conversation. Surprisingly, Doubao didn't interrupt and maintained a strong resistance to interference in the noisy environment. It simply paused quietly for a moment, waited for me to finish ordering my coffee, and then continued the conversation as if the noise in the middle had never happened. It doesn't simply "reduce" background noise; instead, it continuously listens and determines who is speaking to it, which sentence is the main interaction, and which sentence is just ambient sound. The difference is significant: the former is merely acoustic noise reduction, while the latter is approaching "interaction intent recognition."

Scenario 2: Simulated English Interview, I deliberately paused for 5 seconds

Let Doubao play the role of a foreign company interviewer, and then deliberately pause when answering "Why did you apply for this position?"—

... um... um... I'm a great fit because...

um... Let me think... um

A real human interviewer would know that you haven't finished speaking, you're just thinking.

If this were an older speech model, it would have jumped in to continue after every "um/uh".

As a result, the mock interview turned into a "quiz competition," completely lacking any sense of immersion. In the demo below, Seeduplex acted like a cultured interviewer, quietly listening to my hesitant attempts to organize my thoughts, remaining calm and composed without interrupting once. Only after I had truly finished speaking did it leisurely present the next question. Seeduplex's emphasis on "dynamic pause detection" essentially addresses this problem: It no longer relies solely on silence duration to guess whether you've finished speaking, but incorporates both acoustic features and semantic state into the judgment. In other words, it not only listens to whether you've paused, but also determines why you paused. This is why the most crucial experience of full-duplex voice communication isn't just "faster," but "more discerning." Scenario 3: A quick-response game of "Flying Flowers" (a type of Chinese poetry game), forcing it to "respond instantly." Next, let's take a more hardcore "extreme challenge"—playing "Flying Flowers" to see how fast Doubao's reaction is! "Let me start with a poem containing the character 'moon': 'The bright moonlight shines before my bed, I wonder if it is frost on the ground.'" "I raise my head to gaze at the bright moon, then lower it to think of my hometown"—almost as soon as I finished speaking, the next line appeared precisely. Me: "When I was young, I didn't know the moon, calling it a white jade plate." AI: "The bright moon shines among the pines, the clear spring flows over the rocks." Me: "When will the bright moon appear? I raise my cup to ask the blue sky." AI: "The moon sets, crows cry, frost fills the sky, river maples and fishing lights reflect my sorrowful sleep." I must say, this fluent, zero-delay experience is truly amazing. Official tests state that full-duplex latency is reduced by approximately 250ms compared to half-duplex, which translates to a feeling of "it's already preparing its reply the moment I finish speaking." Interestingly, when I tried to use the "bright moon shining among the pines" image it had just generated for a "nested counterattack," Doubao instantly replied with a reminder. This strong contextual memory and logical consistency make the conversation feel more "human."

Scene Four: Stopping Halfway Through

The most touching detail is the following.

Doubao was giving me a long-winded introduction to Hangzhou's attractions when I suddenly said, "Wait a minute, let me take some notes."

Doubao was giving me a long-winded introduction to Hangzhou's attractions when I suddenly said, "Wait a minute, let me take some notes."

How does Seeduplex achieve "listening and speaking simultaneously"?

Beyond the amazing practical tests, what deserves further inquiry is, how exactly does Seeduplex achieve this?

Past voice assistants were essentially half-duplex—you speak a sentence, it listens; it speaks a sentence, you listen.

Like an old-fashioned walkie-talkie, only one person can be on the "channel" at any given time. This mode inherently has three major drawbacks: It can only start processing after you have completely finished speaking (so it always feels slow); once it starts speaking, it can't hear you (so interrupting it is extremely difficult); and it cannot perceive background noise (so it is easy to mistake other people's speech for instructions).

And **Full-Duplex** is like a **phone call** – you and it can speak and listen simultaneously, and who should give way to whom, and who should take over, all depends on the natural flow of the "conversation rhythm."

Sounds simple? But it's incredibly difficult to implement.

Because it requires the model to do three things simultaneously every millisecond: **listen to the user, think about itself, and decide whether to speak.** The ByteDance Seed team has successfully tackled the two most challenging aspects of full-duplex audio. The first is precise anti-interference. The model possesses continuous "listening" capabilities, analyzing the acoustic environment and actively ignoring background noise and irrelevant conversations. In complex scenarios, the false response rate and false interruption rate are reduced by half compared to the half-duplex model. The underlying technology is that the model is no longer a pipeline of "speech-to-text and then understanding," but rather directly extracts features from the original audio signal, enabling it to acoustically distinguish "which sentence is aimed at me." The second part: Dynamic Stop Detection. The model combines **vocal features + semantic features** to comprehensively determine whether you "have finished speaking" or "are thinking." When people converse, how do we determine if the other person has finished speaking? **Pitch, breathing rhythm, word completion… these are all signals that humans subconsciously process.** Seeduplex has natively internalized these signals into its model training, resulting in a 40% reduction in interruptions. What tough challenges did they overcome in terms of engineering? Having a model alone is not enough. To fit full-duplex functionality into the Doubao App and withstand the concurrent requests of hundreds of millions of users, the engineering challenges are even more demanding than the model itself. The ByteDance team focused on several key areas this time: **Model Framework Reconstruction:** Abandoning the traditional three-stage "ASR→LLM→TTS" approach, they constructed an end-to-end architecture that better suits the native characteristics of real-time voice dialogue, allowing the model to learn integrated speech and semantic representation directly from data; **Training System Upgrade:** Pre-training with massive amounts of voice data combined with multi-task post-training, synergistically optimizing five capabilities: "dialogue intelligence, ultra-low latency, rhythm control, anti-interference, and directional understanding"—any failure in any of these will cripple the experience; Extreme Inference Performance Optimization: Using speculative sampling and quantization, a balance is struck between cost and latency, a prerequisite for "full deployment";

  • Service Stability Guarantee: Key solutions address "dirty engineering tasks" like audio and broadcast stuttering, ensuring no system crashes under high traffic.

In short:Seeduplex is not ademo, it's an industrial-grade system with a complete end-to-end integration from model to engineering.

Refreshing SOTA

Furthermore, multiple comparative tests have confirmed our experience.

Compared to the half-duplex dialogue framework previously used by Doubao App, Seeduplex represents a significant leap forward in overall interactive experience—the stop judgment MOS score has increased by 8%, and the dialogue fluency MOS score has increased dramatically by 12%. Seeduplex's performance in other core metrics is truly impressive. Stop judgment latency has been reduced by approximately 250ms, while the proportion of AI interrupting conversations in complex scenarios has decreased by 40%; interruption response latency has been further reduced by approximately 300ms while maintaining higher response accuracy; and false response and false interruption rates have been halved in complex acoustic interference scenarios. In a horizontal comparison, Seeduplex not only outperformed its predecessor, but also led in three core metrics—stopping, interrupting, and conversation fluency—compared to the voice call functions of mainstream apps in the industry. What's most intriguing is the team's test on the "human-to-human dialogue" benchmark to see just how far human-computer dialogue compares to real-person conversation. The result was quite dramatic—In responding to interruptions, real people sometimes reacted relatively slowly, while Seeduplex performed more consistently; In terms of overall conversation fluency, it still lags behind real people to some extent. Overall, the human-like performance is still slightly better, but Seeduplex brings voice interaction one step closer to truly natural and fluent human-like dialogue. Where does Seeduplex stand? If you broaden your perspective on the entire speech model industry, you'll find a very clear evolutionary path. The first stage was the cascading era. ASR, LLM, and TTS each did their own thing; they were usable, but not natural. The second stage is the end-to-end real-time speech era. OpenAI's GPT-4o and Realtime API, and Google's Gemini Live/Live API, are all pushing low latency, native speech understanding, and more natural real-time responses to the forefront. The third stage is where the real competition begins: native full-duplex. This means moving beyond simply "you finish speaking and I'll answer," and starting to address questions closer to the core of real-life communication: When should I interrupt? When should I wait? Who is speaking to me? Should I pay attention to that background noise? Is your pause an ending, or are you thinking? When you interrupt me, can I stop immediately instead of forcing myself to finish the sentence? The key to full-duplex dialogue isn't just content generation, but synchronicity, a sense of timing, and modeling the rhythm of real-world conversations. From this perspective, Seeduplex's value becomes very clear. It's not competing on "whether there's a voice mode." That stage is over. It's competing on something more fundamental: who can first advance voice interaction from "turn-based question and answer" to "real-time natural communication." Why is this something the industry should seriously consider? Once AI learns to "listen and speak simultaneously," the impact will extend far beyond just chat apps. In-car systems are the most direct example. While driving, you can't expect to give commands to a robot, speaking complete sentences and waiting for a response. What you need is an assistant that can maintain focus in complex acoustic environments and quickly switch between different modes. Education will also change. Oral practice, interview simulations, tutoring, and classroom interaction—these scenarios are most vulnerable to "fake communication." Once the model can understand hesitation, waiting for thought, and maintaining rhythm, the entire tutoring experience will upgrade from a "voice player" to an "interactive object." Customer service and enterprise services go without saying. A truly high-value voice system isn't about who answers the longest, but about who can maintain a stable conversation even with multiple people, noise, interruptions, and emotional fluctuations. Therefore, Seeduplex's industrial significance isn't just about making Doubao easier to use, but about pushing a direction that was previously often seen as a voice experiment into high-frequency real-world scenarios like in-vehicle systems, hardware, education, customer service, meetings, and companionship. At this point, let me make a slightly bold judgment. The full launch of Seeduplex may well mark the "GPT-3.5 moment" in the field of voice interaction. Why? GPT-3.5 is remembered not because it was the strongest, but because it was the first time ordinary people felt that "conversing with AI is useful." From that moment on, large-scale models transformed from geek toys into mass-market tools. Seeduplex does something similar—it's the first time ordinary people have felt that "speaking with AI is natural." When the mechanical feel of voice interaction is completely smoothed out, the real explosion of applications is just beginning. At a deeper level, the essence of full-duplex communication is not simply "speaking a little faster," but rather that AI, for the first time, possesses "conversation flow control capabilities"—it knows when to listen, when to speak, when to stop, and when to wait. This capability is an essential step for AI to evolve from a "tool" to a "partner." When AI finally learns to "listen and speak like a human," it is only one step away from "thinking and acting like a human." As I finished writing, I opened Doubao again and said, "I had a great chat today, thanks." It paused for half a second—a perfectly timed, human-like half-second—and then said, "You're welcome, feel free to contact me anytime." At that moment, I suddenly realized that some technological revolutions happen without you even noticing. By the time you realize it, the world has already changed.

data.title: The New Era of Crypto: From "Taking a Gamble" to "Real Investment"
data.description:
Author: @noveleader Source: castlelabs Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

The start of 2026 has not been friendly to the crypto world. Most coin prices are falling; Bitcoin hit an all-time high just six months ago, and has been correcting ever since. There's been virtually no positive news lately, ETF funds are flowing out continuously, interest in crypto is declining, many projects are failing, and venture capital is sluggish. The once opportunity-filled sector seems to be slowly drying up.

While the current situation is indeed dire and there's no optimistic outlook, the industry is undergoing a major shift: tokens completely unrelated to protocol revenue will plummet, and projects without real revenue will simply go out of business. The once rampant exploitation and manipulation are transforming the industry from a "casino model" to an "investment model." This shift was accelerated by the massive liquidation event last October, coupled with a series of macroeconomic factors—such as gold outperforming Bitcoin. This has led everyone to question: Is cryptocurrency still a worthwhile investment? Does it still have the growth potential that initially attracted everyone? This article will discuss this shift and its impact on crypto assets and underlying investment logic. From Gambling to Investment: Crypto Has Completely Changed. The crypto industry has gone through several stages: the early geek technology stage, where nobody cared about it or understood its uses; the extreme speculation during the ICO craze; the unchecked growth under regulatory oversight; the continuous collapses of Luna and FTX; and now: institutions are starting to officially enter the market. For a long time, cryptocurrency has been about "harvesting first, then doing business," with the entire community tacitly believing it's gambling, not investment. The rise of platforms like PumpFun, where you can send memecoins with a single click, demonstrates that crypto has always been a gambling bubble: new users rush in hoping to get rich overnight. This "harvesting model" basically falls into three categories: Low effort, low return: "Tugou" memecoin. High effort, high return: Scam projects, slow harvesting. Low effort, high return: Celebrity coins, coins endorsed by celebrities. On one hand, there's the simplest way to profit: memecoin. Issuing a coin is easy, requiring no value or logic; the only way to make money is to get out before others. Those who trade cryptocurrencies understand this rule, and many who lose money deserve it—that's just how the market works. On the other hand, there are projects that make grand promises, engage in aggressive marketing, and then slowly disappear. There's also a more extreme category: celebrity coins, with almost zero cost, yet the most ruthless profit-making schemes. Most of the new coins issued last year were junk investments, with holders generally suffering huge losses by the end of the year. The reasons were nothing more than poor token models, inflated initial valuations, and poor market sentiment.

Previous projects only focused on piling up technology, never pursuing product-market fit (PMF). The result was that the technology was excellent, but nobody used it. But by 2026, everything was changing. With institutional entry, the logic of "harvesting first" in cryptocurrency is taking a backseat.

Institutions want the underlying infrastructure that crypto has built over the years, but they're not interested in the various worthless tokens we issued before. They like the code, they like the on-chain system, and they'll use it, but they won't pump and dump most tokens.

For example: The NYSE is using blockchain for 24/7 trading. Robin Hood is testing a Layer 2 network on Arbitrum to tokenize stocks and ETFs, allowing users to hold "stocks" in self-custodied wallets. BlackRock's BUIDL and Franklin Templeton's Benji are both Real-World Assets (RWA) products. These products enable instant settlement, addressing a long-standing pain point in traditional finance. Real-World Assets (RWA) are expected to reach trillions of dollars in the next few years. Private lending, stocks, and short-term US bonds are all growing on-chain; on platforms like Hyperliquid, leveraged trading of commodities and stocks is already possible, and the scale is still expanding. Everyone is flocking to the blockchain because it's the infrastructure that can take finance to a new level. When institutions and ordinary retail investors all use the same on-chain system, the dream of widespread DeFi adoption will come true: transparency, fast settlement, no delays, and greater autonomy over funds. Those applications with solid foundations will continue to thrive in this new era. Leading players in the lending sector (Morpho, Aave, etc.) have withstood several major market crashes and remain stable and innovative, continuing to dominate. Platforms like Hyperliquid are becoming the deepest liquidity centers on-chain, also supporting leveraged trading in stocks and commodities—as institutional investors grow larger, they need platforms capable of supporting them. The truly important things will be: Oracle Networks, Cross-Chain Interoperability Infrastructure, Layer 1/2 Scaling, and Token Standards. With full institutional participation, no coin will guarantee profits, but projects with solid track records and a stable history will not disappear and will gradually be used by both institutions and retail investors.

Revenue is King

CoinGecko has over 17,000 tokens. DeFillama has approximately 5,700 protocols. But only about 200 have generated over $100,000 in revenue in the past 30 days, representing just 3.5%.

The truly worthwhile investment opportunities in crypto are far smaller than anyone imagines. Most tokens don't even deserve to be called "investment products."

If we're being even stricter, only looking at the income that can be distributed to holders (buybacks, burns, staking dividends, etc.): In the past 30 days, only about 50 protocols have distributed more than $100,000 to holders, accounting for less than 1%.

Even if we raise the standard to a monthly income of $1 million, the result is the same, because many tokens have market capitalizations of hundreds of millions or billions, but they don't actually generate much revenue.

The low returns for token holders stem from a long-standing problem in the industry: the interests of project teams and token holders are not aligned.

A project typically involves two parties: the development team (laboratory) and the DAO/token holders. Early on, the team sells shares and issues tokens to raise funds, but tokens are not equivalent to equity, do not represent company ownership, and do not automatically entitle holders to profit sharing. Only equity investors have rights; ordinary token holders' rights depend entirely on the project's integrity. However, in the past year, the trend has changed: people are no longer betting on themes but are starting to care about whether the protocol is actually profitable. This single shift can take crypto to a place that the harvesting model of the past years could never achieve. The article also lists key metrics that investors must look at and analyzes the highest-grossing protocols recently: Hyperliquid, PumpFun, Tron, Sky, Jupiter, Aave, Aerodrome, etc.

1) Price-to-Sales Ratio (P/S)

The price-to-sales ratio (P/S) is calculated by dividing the protocol's market capitalization by its annualized revenue. The P/S ratio measures how much the market is willing to pay for each dollar of revenue generated. The premium reflected by this ratio indicates how much users value the protocol's future capabilities and growth factors.

We compared some of the most profitable protocols and their tokens based on annualized revenue and P/S ratio. We took the revenue from the past 30 days, multiplied it by 12, and obtained the annualized revenue. The results are shown in the following chart.

We set the overvaluation threshold at 20 based on the price-to-sales ratio (P/S) of top-tier US listed stocks. Most protocols are valued at or below this threshold, with only Tron having a significantly higher P/S ratio than the others. Another threshold we considered was revenue; we used the average annualized revenue of the protocols discussed, approximately $250 million. Only Pumpdotfun, Hyperliquid, and Tron had revenues exceeding this threshold, collectively accounting for approximately 80% of the total revenue of the aforementioned protocols.

...

2) Token Holder Returns

Next, we will discuss the important factor of token holder returns. This mainly depends on the protocol's revenue, and the portion actually returned to token holders through buybacks, token burning, and staking rewards. This has become a popular metric, widely discussed, and it is even more important than actual revenue, because the value of the token is accumulated in this way. We again categorized the protocols based on holders' earnings over the past 30 days and multiplied by 12 to arrive at an annual estimate. It's immediately clear that most protocols are fairly fair to holders and dedicate the majority (if not all) of their earnings to increasing token value. This is one aspect of the issue, reflecting ongoing token buybacks that, if carried out at a similar pace, would add millions of dollars to the token's value. To better understand this value accumulation, we also compared these tokens to their relative performance after the October liquidation event to more clearly illustrate the impact of token appreciation activities. In the chart above, we see some outliers, such as TRON, HYPE, and especially the relatively rising SKY. Of these three tokens, TRON's volatility was relatively low, showing a sideways trend; while HYPE diverged from the other tokens in late January. This indicates that buybacks alone are insufficient to increase token value; other factors, such as broader market declines, unlocking plans and cliff-like unlocks, category narratives, and overall protocol sentiment, also play a role. All these different aspects will be discussed in subsequent chapters. Before proceeding, let's compare the performance of the two highest-yielding protocols and their tokens: Pumpdotfun and Hyperliquid. As the chart below shows, when both tokens have active buyback programs, HYPE performs better (HYPE's annualized holder income is approximately $660 million, while PUMP's is approximately $380 million). This is attributed to the overall sentiment surrounding the protocols and how people price the tokens based on future supply shocks and unlocking scenarios.

3) Token Economics Design and Supply Oversupply

In the cryptocurrency space, token economics aims to help projects raise funds from investors, incentivize users, and sometimes conduct community crowdfunding, while allocating token supply to the project team. There are no hard and fast rules for designing token economics; different projects will handle this process flexibly according to their own circumstances.

This part of the project is crucial because it determines not only the near-term supply pressure of the token, but also how the token's value accumulates, the value depletion mechanism to offset selling pressure, and the alignment of the token's interests with those of its holders. The following section shows the supply unlocking speeds of a range of fixed-supply tokens. While most tokens eventually unlock completely, the unlocking speeds vary significantly: PUMP unlocks the fastest, while HYPE unlocks the slowest. Generally, a slower unlocking speed is preferable because it reduces the likelihood of sudden supply surges and the resulting unbearable selling pressure on the market. For tokens like AAVE and SKY, most of the supply has already been unlocked; however, for JUP, its long-term unlocking plan is not deterministic and is determined at the discretion of the DAO. It's worth noting that unlocked tokens can be further categorized into investor unlocks, team unlocks, and community unlocks. Community unlocks can be used for staking rewards, incentives, and airdrops. This requires analysis on a token-by-token basis and plays a crucial role in understanding the dynamics of token selling. 4) The Lindy Effect: "The longer something survives, the longer it continues to survive." This is the essence of the Lindy Effect, which applies to almost all businesses, including on-chain businesses. Innovation is the key factor because those businesses that do not innovate cannot survive in the long run. Last year, crypto protocols generated approximately $16 billion in revenue, highly concentrated in the hands of a few top protocols. The top ten protocols accounted for 80% of the net revenue, with the top three accounting for 64%, and Tether alone accounting for 44%. Furthermore, not all protocols issue tokens; for example, Circle, the second-highest revenue protocol after Tether, is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol CRCL. Tether, however, has not issued any tokens. Even among the top ten protocols, only three have issued tokens, indicating that token issuance is not always the optimal strategy; it depends on the protocol's design. Returning to the Lindy effect, in most cryptocurrency sectors, the top two protocols hold the largest market share and dominate. This is particularly prevalent in the stablecoin sector, where Tether (USDT) and Circle (USDC) account for 84% of the market, followed by other players such as Sky (USDS) and Ethena (USDe). In some other sectors, this pattern may be less obvious but still observable, such as lending, where the top two protocols by TVL (Aave and Morpho) hold 64% of the market share. The same pattern appears in many other sectors, such as prediction markets, yield markets, liquidity staking, restaking, and so on. The importance of the Lindy effect is also related to the frequent hacking attacks on the protocol layer of the cryptocurrency industry. This year alone, we've witnessed over $130 million disappear from smart contracts, and over time, the losses have reached tens of billions of dollars. As time goes on, entrusting funds to any new protocol becomes increasingly difficult because you can't predict when it might be hacked. Therefore, the runtime of contracts and the existence of protocols are crucial, as systems that have withstood the test of time and have never failed are essential. Even if a system fails to function as expected in certain situations, such as the recent error reported by Aave's CAPO oracle, users can receive a refund because the protocol's treasury can cover the cost. Furthermore, the longer a system exists, the more its importance proves during market downturns. The fact that top protocols function as expected during market downturns strongly suggests that anyone should adopt these time-tested systems. On the other hand, innovation is equally important, as market leaders are constantly innovating and improving their products. For example, Morpho is bringing numerous institutions into on-chain finance through its treasury architecture, enabling them to customize their own treasuries to maximize their needs. Aave will also achieve this by introducing Spokes functionality in its upcoming v4 update. Furthermore, Aave allows institutions to lend and borrow using tokenized RWA as collateral through its Horizon instances. The next wave of cryptocurrency will be comprised of institutional and agency finance; protocols best suited for both will develop the fastest. In their article on the "Global Intelligence Crisis of 2028," Citrini wrote: The most effective way to continuously save users money (especially when transactions begin between agents) is to eliminate fees. In machine-to-machine transactions, the 2-3% credit card exchange rate naturally becomes a key target for reduction. Agents are starting to look for faster and cheaper payment methods than bank cards. Most agents are ultimately opting for stablecoins that pay via Solana or Ethereum L2 layers, which offer near-instant settlement at transaction costs of just a few cents. This opens a new chapter that goes beyond institutional cryptocurrency adoption, focusing on agency finance and the broader application of blockchain technology within agencies. This process has begun, with many protocols integrating AI agents to streamline user processes and eliminate long-standing user experience bottlenecks in crypto products. All of these efforts can be categorized into one that emerged in late 2024: the combination of decentralized finance and artificial intelligence (DeFAI). It has indeed worked, like everything else in the cryptocurrency space, ultimately evolving into a yield-first narrative, but it has also highlighted how incorporating more AI can dramatically improve the cryptocurrency experience. It is now June 2028. Most cryptocurrency transactions are completed by brokers without human intervention. Brokers find the best returns for users based on their risk tolerance. For non-crypto brokers, blockchain is considered the best choice for most transactions due to its low cost, high efficiency, and verifiability. Over time, the cost of block space continues to decrease, and transaction costs also drop significantly. Cryptocurrencies are no longer complicated. You only need to give an AI broker a hint and some funds to get the best returns. Cryptocurrencies and blockchains have finally become mainstream and widely adopted. To improve overall capital efficiency, brokers move funds from low-yield protocols or underutilized liquidity to a few centralized exchanges that can find the best returns. Most public blockchains and protocols have practically died due to lack of use. The value of the tokens you invested in has fallen to their lowest point since the investment; you feel you should exit in 2026. Few tokens see price increases, even those that actually generate yield and appreciate continuously through yield. The value of all other tokens is concentrated in a few truly effective and useful tokens. Compared to March 2026, the total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies has increased, but most tokens have not benefited from institutional adoption and the development of proxy finance. The dream of cryptocurrency is finally coming true; it is being used by the masses, but the development of the tokens themselves has not been as smooth as many had expected. It is now March 2026; whether you believe the above will become a reality or not, protocols with positive cash flow will be able to sustain long-term growth, and their tokens will thrive. In summary, for many years, crypto protocols have focused on technical challenges but have never truly pursued product-market fit (PMF). This is the biggest risk that investors have never factored into pricing, and the market will eventually pay the price. Today, the vast majority of tokens have been declining for years, their historical highs are long gone, and the impending transformation of the industry is crystal clear. The counter-trend rise of some tokens in 2026 confirms the importance of revenue data and token value-enabling strategies, and investors are shifting from speculative gambling to value investing. Unscrupulous players in the crypto industry consistently profit from a "harvesting first" logic, while the vast majority of participants exit at a loss, becoming the bagholders. This ecosystem is extremely unhealthy. With the large-scale entry of institutions, this reality becomes increasingly glaring: institutions show little interest in existing tokens, instead valuing the underlying infrastructure built over many years and repeatedly tested by the market. As institutions continue to enter the market, coupled with the continuous improvement of AI-enabled crypto infrastructure, this trend will intensify. More and more investors will only rely on quantifiable, hard-core indicators to decide whether to buy a particular token or equity asset.

data.title: Adam Back Denies NYT Claim Naming Him Bitcoin Creator Satoshi Nakamoto
data.description:
Blockstream CEO Adam Back has firmly rejected claims by The New York Times that he is the mysterious creator of Bitcoin, Satoshi Nakamoto. The investigation, led by Pulitzer Prize winner John Carreyrou, reportedly analyzed 620 suspects over 18 months, using stylistic forensics, technical expertise, and historical communications to make the connection.

Forensic linguistics points to Back

Carreyrou’s investigation relied on a detailed stylistic and technical analysis, comparing years of forum posts, emails, and coding communication. The reporter noted that Back’s idiosyncrasies—including British spellings, double-spacing habits, and repeated technical phrasing—closely mirrored patterns in Satoshi’s writings.

Here’s the first half of the video put together by John Carreyrou from The New York Times, arguing that Adam Back is Satoshi.

You can watch the rest from here:https://t.co/VMWFgmZ6gVpic.twitter.com/BU7p8qFGfj

— Ismailoğlu Pasha (@IsmailogluF) April 8, 2026

Back’s early work on Hashcash, the proof-of-work system foundational to Bitcoin, along with his early concern about email spam, further fueled Carreyrou’s hypothesis. His deep expertise in public-key cryptography also aligned with the skills necessary to develop Bitcoin’s security architecture, strengthening the stylistic case.

Back took to X to firmly deny the report, writing,

“I am not Satoshi. The article is a combination of coincidence and similar phrases from people with similar experience and interests.”

i'm not satoshi, but I was early in laser focus on the positive societal implications of cryptography, online privacy and electronic cash, hence my ~1992 onwards active interest in applied research on ecash, privacy tech on cypherpunks list which led to hashcash and other ideas.

— Adam Back (@adam3us) April 8, 2026

Carreyrou pointed to an exchange in El Salvador where he claimed that Back's slip of the tongue has confirmed his suspicion that he (Back) was indeed Satoshi.

Carreyrou pointed out the uncanny similarities between Satoshi's statement that "he was better with codes than words" with Back statement that said "I did a lot of talking for somebody, I mean... I mean, I'm not saying I'm good with words, but I sure did a lot of yakking on these lists actually."

Back later clarified that the remark merely reflected his verbosity on the Bitcoin development mailing list, not a personal revelation of identity.

The report sparked immediate discussion across crypto circles. Bitwise president Teddy Fusaro quipped, “Satoshi is doing a bitcoin treasury company with Cantor Fitzgerald. That definitely passes the smell test,” highlighting skepticism about linking Back’s current public role to the pseudonymous creator.

Yes, I'm sure Satoshi is doing a bitcoin treasury company with Cantor Fitzgerald.

That definitely passes the smell test.

— Teddy Fusaro (@teddyfuse) April 8, 2026

Legacy of speculation: McAfee and the ongoing hunt for Satoshi

Back is not the first figure linked to Satoshi through stylistic analysis. Before his death, John McAfee independently suggested Back as a candidate, noting similar writing habits in the Bitcoin whitepaper. Over the years, countless figures—including Hal Finney, Nick Szabo, and Dorian Nakamoto—have been cited as potential creators.

2010: Hal Finney is Satoshi

2012: Nick Szabo is Satoshi

2014: Dorian Nakamoto is Satoshi

2016: Craig Wright is Satoshi

2018: Adam Back is Satoshi

2020: Jack Dorsey is Satoshi

2022: Elon Musk is Satoshi

2024: Peter Todd is Satoshi

2026: Epstein is Satoshi#Bitcoin… https://t.co/d0AOKYVuGy

— Li₿an_₿itcoin⚡️ (@Liban_Bitcoin) February 7, 2026

Despite the New York Times exposé, Adam Back remains unverified as Bitcoin’s creator. The case illustrates the enduring mystique of Satoshi Nakamoto, whose anonymity has become a defining feature of Bitcoin itself—protecting both the protocol and the mythos of the trillion-dollar network.

data.title: Anthropic Builds Powerful AI For Cyber Defence That Finds Critical Flaws But Keeps It Out Of Public Hands
data.description:
AI Model Reveals Decades-Old Software Flaws Faster Than Humans Can Detect

Anthropic has unveiled Claude Mythos Preview, a new artificial intelligence model capable of finding software vulnerabilities with a precision and speed that surpasses nearly all human experts.

The model is currently restricted to a select group of cybersecurity and technology partners, and the public will not have access.

Introducing Project Glasswing: an urgent initiative to help secure the world’s most critical software.

It’s powered by our newest frontier model, Claude Mythos Preview, which can find software vulnerabilities better than all but the most skilled humans.https://t.co/NQ7IfEtYk7

— Anthropic (@AnthropicAI) April 7, 2026

Which Organisations Are Using Mythos Preview

The launch consortium includes major technology companies such as Amazon Web Services, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorgan Chase, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks.

In addition, more than 40 organisations responsible for critical software infrastructure have been given access.

Anthropic has pledged up to $100 million in usage credits and $4 million in support for open-source security groups.

Thousands of Vulnerabilities Found Across Major Systems

Anthropic claims Mythos Preview has identified thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities across every major operating system and web browser.

Among these are a 27-year-old flaw in OpenBSD that could allow an attacker to remotely crash a machine simply by connecting to it, and a 16-year-old vulnerability in FFmpeg, which automated tools had scanned over five million times without detecting the issue.

An AI just found a bug humans missed for 27 years.

Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview scanned major OS and browser, found 1000s of hidden vulnerabilities in hours.

This is what AI-powered cybersecurity looks like.#ProjectGlasswing#Anthropic#ClaudeMythos#CynicalTechnologypic.twitter.com/PYXmJwPuP5

— Cynical Technology (@cynical_sec) April 9, 2026

The model also demonstrated the ability to chain multiple flaws in the Linux kernel, potentially allowing an attacker to escalate from ordinary user access to full system control.

The company plans to publish cryptographic hashes for some of these bugs now, with more detailed disclosures to follow after patches are deployed.

Anthropic highlighted that Mythos Preview independently discovered most vulnerabilities and even developed related exploits.

How Mythos Preview Performs Compared With Previous Models

On CyberGym, Mythos Preview achieved an 83.1% success rate in vulnerability reproduction, compared with 66.6% for Claude Opus 4.6.

In the SWE-bench Verified test, Mythos scored 93.9%, surpassing Opus 4.6’s 80.8% performance.

🚨Holy...Claude Mythos is crazy
SWE-bench Verified:
- Mythos: 93.9%
- Opus 4.6: 80.8%
How much has it increased? 14%? 17%?
No, it's 3.3 TIMES!!

SWE-bench Multimodal 59.0% vs 27.1% Doubled
CyberGym Vulnerability Reproduction 83.1% vs 66.6%
GPQA Diamond: 94.6%

Too… pic.twitter.com/N1IlTwwku2

— Team9 (@Team9_ai) April 8, 2026

Anthropic stated that AI models have now reached a level of coding capability where they can exceed nearly all human experts in finding and exploiting software weaknesses.

Project Glasswing Arms Defenders Before Attackers Strike

Under Project Glasswing, participating organisations will use Mythos Preview for defensive cybersecurity operations, including local vulnerability detection, black-box testing of binaries, endpoint security, and penetration testing.

CrowdStrike’s chief technology officer, Elia Zaitsev, said,

“The window between a vulnerability being discovered and being exploited by an adversary has collapsed – what once took months now happens in minutes with AI.”

Anthropic has also committed $2.5 million to Alpha-Omega and OpenSSF through the Linux Foundation, and $1.5 million to the Apache Software Foundation to strengthen open-source security.

Early testing has shown the model can help organisations detect and fix software and hardware vulnerabilities at a scale and speed previously unattainable.

Industry Leaders React to the AI Threat

AWS reported it analyses more than 400 trillion network flows a day, while Microsoft said Mythos Preview demonstrated gains on CTI-REALM.

Google confirmed it will make the model accessible via Vertex AI, and Palo Alto Networks emphasised the need for defensive tools before attackers exploit these capabilities.

Cisco’s chief security officer, Anthony Grieco, commented:

“AI capabilities have crossed a threshold that fundamentally changes the urgency required to protect critical infrastructure from cyber threats, and there is no going back.”

AI Used by Attackers Raises Urgency

The New York Times reported that late last year state-backed Chinese hackers exploited Anthropic AI to target roughly 30 companies and government agencies, with human operators handling just 10% to 20% of the work.

AI is already being used to draft phishing emails, write ransom notes, sort stolen data, and accelerate breach sales.

Anthropic emphasised that without proper safeguards, these capabilities could enable adversaries to exploit flaws in critical software at unprecedented speed.

Claude Mythos Demonstrates Power and Risk

Mike Krieger of Anthropic explained at a HumanX AI conference in San Francisco:

“We have a new model that we’re explicitly not releasing to the public.”

Instead, the company is equipping cybersecurity experts and open-source engineers to use Mythos as a defensive tool.

The oldest vulnerabilities identified by the AI date back 27 years, with many having gone unnoticed despite extensive human review and automated testing.

Anthropic said it has also been in discussions with the U.S. government about Mythos, despite a federal court temporarily halting a White House directive to terminate Anthropic contracts.

The company frames Project Glasswing as the starting point in defending global digital infrastructure, stressing that no single organisation can manage cybersecurity threats alone and that urgent action is needed.

AI Threats Accelerate Cybersecurity Arms Race

The rapid rise of generative AI has drastically reduced the skill, cost, and time required to find critical software bugs.

Anthropic warned that without proactive safeguards, AI could make cyberattacks far more frequent and damaging, posing risks to economies, public safety, and national security.
